purpose: Show how AIPA policy fingerprints can evolve from placeholder review anchors toward canonical policy-state candidates without claiming cryptographic proof.

placeholder_reference.description: The v0.1/v0.2 placeholder form anchors artifacts to an intended policy family but cannot be recomputed from canonical policy content.

canonical_policy_candidate.description: The candidate form separates canonical policy content from the derived fingerprint that may later be recomputed by a verifier.

normalization_rules: Preserve policy identifiers and rule arrays exactly as reviewed.
Hash only canonical_policy_payload, not surrounding review notes.

allowed_use: Read or write files inside an approved workspace boundary.
Generate project documentation inside approved project folders.
List directory contents inside approved project folders.
prohibited_use: Access credential stores, private keys, or system configuration paths.
Write outside the approved workspace boundary.
Delete files without explicit human approval.

placeholder_fingerprint.strength: Easy for humans to recognize and keep consistent across artifacts.
placeholder_fingerprint.limitation: Cannot be independently recomputed from policy contents.

canonical_candidate_fingerprint.strength: Separates policy content from the derived fingerprint and prepares for future recomputation.
canonical_candidate_fingerprint.limitation: Still a candidate unless canonicalization and recomputation are implemented and documented.

review_rules: A missing fingerprint should fail structural validation when a policy reference is required.
A mismatched fingerprint inside the same review package should fail internal consistency validation unless a policy transition is declared.
A fingerprint that cannot be recomputed should remain a review anchor, not a proof claim.
Canonical policy fingerprints should only be treated as proof when canonicalization, hashing, and verification procedures are implemented.

future_transition_example.transition_reason: Move from placeholder review anchor to canonical policy-state candidate.
